request.question: Which Canadian singer sang " something stronger" in the musical applause?

title: Sweden in the Eurovision Song Contest 1965
Applause, Applause
The Singer Sang His Song
Jumbo (Bee Gees song)
Applause (Bonnie Franklin song)
Anna Russell
Spin It Again! Live in Munich
Leland Palmer (actress)
Jennifer Gillis
Len Cariou
sentences: After a year of absence, Sweden competed in the Eurovision Song Contest 1965 with the opera singer Ingvar Wixell, and the song "Absent Friend" (originally "Annorstädes vals") composed by Dag Wirén.
For the first time, one singer sang all the songs in the national final, and Ingvar was also the first male singer to represent Sweden.
It was the first ESC entry that was not performed in the country's native language; this led to the introduction of the language rule the following year.
Applause, Applause was a Canadian musical variety television miniseries which aired on CBC Television in 1974.
"The Singer Sang His Song" is a song written by Barry, Robin and Maurice Gibb and recorded by the English rock group Bee Gees, released in early 1968 as a single along with Jumbo.
In some countries the song was the B-side of Jumbo but in others they were promoted as a double A-side.
"Jumbo" is a song released by the Bee Gees, written by Barry, Robin & Maurice Gibb.
It was released as a double A side with "The Singer Sang His Song" but featured as the lead track in some territories.

"Applause" is the title song from the 1970 Broadway musical "Applause", with music by Charles Strouse and lyrics by Lee Adams, originally performed by Bonnie Franklin, who originated the role of Bonnie in the musical, and recorded as a single with orchestra and chorus conducted by Donald Pippin.
The single was released with a B-side featuring the star of the production, Lauren Bacall, making her musical theatre debut, performing "Something Greater" together with Len Cariou.

The single's popularity led to Franklin's being invited to perform it on the 24th Tony Awards broadcast on television, where the show gained Best Musical, Bacall Best Leading Actress in a Musical, but Bonnie Franklin missed out on the best supporting actress to Melba Moore.
Anna Russell (born Anna Claudia Russell-Brown; 27 December 191118 October 2006) was an English–Canadian singer and comedian.
She gave many concerts in which she sang and played comic musical sketches on the piano.

Among her best-known works are her concert performances and famous recordings of "The Ring of the Nibelungs (An Analysis)" – a humorous 22-minute synopsis of Richard Wagner's "Der Ring des Nibelungen" – and (on the same album) her parody "How to Write Your Own Gilbert and Sullivan Opera."
Spin It Again!
Live in Munich is a live album and video by the Canadian band Saga.
It was recorded in 2012 at the end of their 20/20 tour and celebrates the reunion with original singer Michael Sadler.
It was also the first Saga release featuring their new drummer Mike Thorne.
Besides three songs from "20/20", it includes mainly songs from the first five Saga albums.
The concert was released on different formats by edel records/earMUSIC, including a double CD, DVD (Super Jewel Box packaging) and Blu-Ray.
The video formats also include a twenty-minute tour documentary.
The CD has edited applause between the main set and the encores, but otherwise the performance is identical.
As is typical for Saga shows in Germany, Michael Sadler does some German announcements.
The concert also includes a litte "unplugged" set.
After returning to the stage, Sadler says "What shall I play?"
He starts on "Images (Chapter 1)", then stops, goes into "No Regrets (Chapter 5)", stops again and finally declares "I got it!"
before launching into "Tired World (Chapter 6)".
Leland Palmer (born June 16, 1945, Port Washington, New York) is an American actress, dancer, and singer who has appeared on stage, in motion pictures, and on television.
She appeared on Broadway in "Bajour", "A Joyful Noise", "Hello, Dolly!
", "Applause", and "Pippin".
Palmer received two Tony Award nominations: in 1967 for featured actress in a musical (Miss Jimmie in "A Joyful Noise"), and in 1973 for actress in a musical (Fastrada in "Pippin").
She appeared as Viola in the rock musical "Your Own Thing" during a highly regarded run at the Huntington Hartford Theater in Los Angeles in August 1968.

Jennifer Kristine Gillis (born 26 April 1996) is a Canadian singer, dancer and actress with an extensive resume in musical theatre, television, singing, radio, recording, and animated voice-over work.

Gillis is most noted for performing in Sir Andrew Lloyd Webber's CBC TV reality show "Over the Rainbow"-a competition to be cast as the leading role of Dorothy Gale in Lloyd Webber's forthcoming production of "The Wizard of Oz" in Toronto, Ontario.
Being the youngest aspiring singer in the competition, she singularly represented her province of British Columbia and as a result was named the Top 6th musical theatre performer in all of Canada.
Since "Over the Rainbow", Gillis sang the Canadian national anthem "O Canada" for the Prime Minister of Canada, Stephen Harper on Parliament Hill in Ottawa, Ontario on Canada Day in 2013.
She aspires to become a Broadway actress someday and wants to dedicate her life to performing.
Leonard Joseph "Len" Cariou (born September 30, 1939) is a Canadian actor, best known for his portrayal of Sweeney Todd in the original cast of "", which earned him a Tony award.
He currently plays the patriarch, Henry Reagan, NYPD Police Commissioner (retired), in the multi-generational television series "Blue Bloods" on CBS.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: Which Canadian singer sang " something stronger" in the musical applause?
hop1_docs: Applause, Applause | Applause, Applause was a Canadian musical variety television miniseries which aired on CBC Television in 1974.

Applause (Bonnie Franklin song) | "Applause" is the title song from the 1970 Broadway musical "Applause", with music by Charles Strouse and lyrics by Lee Adams, originally performed by Bonnie Franklin, who originated the role of Bonnie in the musical, and recorded as a single with orchestra and chorus conducted by Donald Pippin.  The single was released with a B-side featuring the star of the production, Lauren Bacall, making her musical theatre debut, performing "Something Greater" together with Len Cariou.  The single's popularity led to Franklin's being invited to perform it on the 24th Tony Awards broadcast on television, where the show gained Best Musical, Bacall Best Leading Actress in a Musical, but Bonnie Franklin missed out on the best supporting actress to Melba Moore.

Siti binti Saad | Siti binti Saad (1880–1950) was a pioneering artist in the taraab genre of east African music.  In an era in which male singers predominated, she was a pioneer as a woman singer in the genre: she was the first woman in East Africa to record her music in an album.  In contrast to previous singers who sang in Arabic, she sang in Swahili.  She sang in cities of the coast of Tanganyika and Zanzibar.

Parichay (singer) | Parichay (born August 22) is an Indo-Canadian singer, composer, and music producer.  He has released three albums and collaborated in Bollywood film songs including a remix of "Yamla Pagla Deewana" and "O Meri Chandni" from the film "Chaar Din Ki Chandni" which he sang with Sunidhi Chauhan.  In 2016, he composed music for the Bollywood film "Loveshhuda".

Applause Cheer Boo Hiss | Applause Cheer Boo Hiss is an EP by Canadian indie rock band Land of Talk, released on April 4, 2006 on Dependent Music in Canada.  It would later be released on Rebel Group Records in the United States on March 20, 2007.

Applause (musical) | Applause is a musical with a book by Betty Comden and Adolph Green, lyrics by Lee Adams, and music by Charles Strouse.  The musical is based on the 1950 film "All About Eve" and the short story on which the movie is based, Mary Orr's "The Wisdom of Eve".  The story centers on aging star Margo Channing, who innocently takes a fledgling actress under her wing, unaware that the ruthless Eve is plotting to steal her career and her man.

Stronger (Carlene Carter album) | Stronger is an album released in 2008 by American country music singer Carlene Carter.  Included is "I'm So Cool," a song originally recorded on Carter's 1980 album, "Musical Shapes".
response.summary_1:
- Fact: [2] quotes that the single's B-side featured Lauren Bacall "performing 'Something Greater' together with Len Cariou."  
- Fact: [2] states "'Applause' ... originally performed by Bonnie Franklin" (orig. performer of the title song).  
- Fact: [6] paraphrase: Applause is the Broadway musical based on All About Eve (gives context that song list/credits belong to that musical).  
- Fact: [4] paraphrase: Parichay is an Indo-Canadian singer (present in passages but not linked to Applause).

Bridge entities / ambiguities preventing a definitive answer:
- Ambiguity A: The question asks about "something stronger" but passages show a song titled "Something Greater" — unclear if the user meant "Something Greater" (possible misremembering) or a different song "Something Stronger".
- Missing fact B: Len Cariou is named as a performer on the B-side, but the passages do not state his nationality or whether he is credited as a "Canadian singer" on this recording.
- Missing fact C: The official Applause (musical) song list/credits in the provided passages do not confirm a song titled "Something Stronger" or the full performer credits.

Prioritized next-hop retrieval targets (what to fetch and which exact field/section to extract):
1. Fetch page: "Applause (Bonnie Franklin song)" — extract the single release details / B-side credits (look for exact quoted phrase, performer credits, and recording credits for "Something Greater"/B-side). Purpose: confirm exact song title on the B-side and the named performers.
2. Fetch page: "Len Cariou" (biography page) — extract nationality, primary occupation (actor/singer), and discography/recording credits or a mention of performing "Something Greater" with Lauren Bacall. Purpose: confirm whether Len Cariou is Canadian and credited as singer on that recording.
3. Fetch page: "Applause (musical)" — extract the official song list and cast/recording credits (section headings: "Songs", "Original cast recording", or "Musical numbers"). Purpose: verify presence/official title of the song (Something Greater vs Something Stronger) and performer assignments.
4. Fetch page: "Lauren Bacall" — extract discography/musical performances or the specific note that she performed "Something Greater" (to corroborate the single's performers).
5. If ambiguity about the title remains, fetch a targeted search/disambiguation for the exact phrase "Something Stronger" + "Applause" (or "Something Stronger" song) to determine if a different song with that title exists and its performer(s).

Do not answer yet: the supplied passages do not unambiguously establish a Canadian singer who sang "something stronger." Fetch the prioritized pages above to resolve the song-title mismatch and to confirm the Canadian performer identity.
response.hop2_query: Len Cariou "Something Greater" Applause B-side performer nationality
hop2_docs: Applause (Bonnie Franklin song) | "Applause" is the title song from the 1970 Broadway musical "Applause", with music by Charles Strouse and lyrics by Lee Adams, originally performed by Bonnie Franklin, who originated the role of Bonnie in the musical, and recorded as a single with orchestra and chorus conducted by Donald Pippin.  The single was released with a B-side featuring the star of the production, Lauren Bacall, making her musical theatre debut, performing "Something Greater" together with Len Cariou.  The single's popularity led to Franklin's being invited to perform it on the 24th Tony Awards broadcast on television, where the show gained Best Musical, Bacall Best Leading Actress in a Musical, but Bonnie Franklin missed out on the best supporting actress to Melba Moore.

Len Cariou | Leonard Joseph "Len" Cariou (born September 30, 1939) is a Canadian actor, best known for his portrayal of Sweeney Todd in the original cast of "", which earned him a Tony award.  He currently plays the patriarch, Henry Reagan, NYPD Police Commissioner (retired), in the multi-generational television series "Blue Bloods" on CBS.

Michael Raver | Michael Raver is an American actor, playwright, journalist and model.  He was born in New York City.  He had his stage debut in Ellen McLaughlin's adaptation of "The Persians" at National Actor's Theater in 2007 alongside Len Cariou, Michael Stuhlbarg and Michael Potts.  He portrayed physicist Richard Feynman in the film "How We Built the Bomb".

The Four Seasons (1981 film) | The Four Seasons is a 1981 romantic comedy film written and directed by and starring Alan Alda, co-starring Carol Burnett, Len Cariou, Sandy Dennis, Rita Moreno, Jack Weston and Bess Armstrong.
Émilie Cariou | Émilie Cariou is a French politician representing La République En Marche!  She was elected to the French National Assembly on 18 June 2017, representing the department of Meuse.

One Man (film) | One Man is a Canadian drama film, released in 1977.  Directed by Robin Spry, the film stars Len Cariou as Jason Brady, a television journalist in Montreal who is investigating a chemical leak from a local factory which has poisoned a number of children.

Teleprompter | A teleprompter is a display device that prompts the person speaking with an electronic visual text of a speech or script.  Using a teleprompter is similar to using cue cards.  The screen is in front of, and usually below, the lens of a professional video camera, and the words on the screen are reflected to the eyes of the presenter using a sheet of clear glass or a specially prepared beam splitter.  Light from the performer passes through the front side of the glass into the lens, while a shroud surrounding the lens and the back side of the glass prevents unwanted light from entering the lens.
response.summary_2: Len Cariou, a Canadian actor, performed "Something Greater" with Lauren Bacall on the B-side of the "Applause" single. Lauren Bacall made her musical-theatre debut on that recording.
response.answer: Len Cariou — he performed "Something Greater" with Lauren Bacall (the B-side of the "Applause" single).
